title: Helixanthera sessiliflora
intro: Helixanthera sessiliflora, also known as the yellow mistletoe or yellow birdlime, is a species of flowering plant in the family Loranthaceae, characterized by its yellow flowers and sticky sap. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various health conditions, including fever, rheumatism, and respiratory issues, due to its potential anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of Helixanthera sessiliflora include alkaloids, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which have been shown to possess antioxidant, antiviral, and anti-cancer activities. In traditional herbal medicine, Helixanthera sessiliflora is commonly prepared as a decoction, tea, or tincture, and is used to treat a range of health conditions, including fever, cough, and sore throat.
taxonomy: Helixanthera sessiliflora is a parasitic flowering plant belonging to the family Loranthaceae. This family is part of the order Santalales, which consists of parasitic and hemiparasitic plants. Loranthaceae is a family of about 70 genera and 900 species, commonly known as mistletoes. The plant is native to tropical and subtropical regions in Asia, with specific distribution in Southeast Asia and the Indian subcontinent. Its classification reflects its unique parasitic lifestyle and close association with host plants.
constituents: Helixanthera sessiliflora contains several active constituents, including alkaloids and terpenoids. These compounds have been found to have potential biological activities, including antimicrobial and antiparasitic properties. The alkaloids present in Helixanthera sessiliflora may also exhibit anti-inflammatory and antiviral effects. Research is ongoing to fully understand the pharmacological properties of these active constituents. Further studies are necessary to isolate and purify these compounds for potential medicinal applications.

side_effects: Helixanthera sessiliflora, a parasitic plant, can cause adverse effects due to its toxicity. Ingesting or handling the plant can lead to skin irritation, allergic reactions, and gastrointestinal issues. The plant's toxic compounds can also cause respiratory problems and eye irritation. There is limited research on the long-term effects of exposure to Helixanthera sessiliflora, but it is essential to exercise caution when handling it. Further studies are needed to fully understand the side effects of this plant.
